## Preprocessing is part of the model, not a cleanup prelude

Raw tabular data is rarely ready for modelling.
Missing values, categorical variables, inconsistent scales, and rare or extreme
values all change how a model behaves.
That means preprocessing is not a cosmetic step before the "real" machine-learning
work starts.
It is part of the modelling workflow itself.

In `TEM 0052`, students should treat preprocessing choices as assumptions that can
change both predictive performance and interpretability.

## Split first, then fit preprocessing

The most important preprocessing rule is chronological rather than mathematical:
split the data before you fit anything that learns from the feature distribution.

That includes:

- imputers that estimate replacement values,
- encoders that learn category sets,
- scalers that estimate means and standard deviations,
- dimensionality-reduction steps such as PCA.

If those steps are fit on the full dataset before the split, the workflow leaks test
information into training.
The code may still run, but the evaluation becomes too optimistic.

## The common preprocessing tasks solve different problems

The usual preprocessing tasks are not interchangeable.
Each one answers a different question:

- imputation handles missing information,
- encoding converts categorical structure into numeric form a model can use,
- scaling keeps large-unit variables from dominating distance- or variance-based
  methods,
- outlier handling decides whether extreme values are signal, noise, or a data-quality
  issue.

The important teaching point is that there is no universal checklist that should be
applied blindly.
Students should be able to justify why a step is needed for a given dataset and a
given downstream model.

## Pipelines make the workflow reproducible

Manual preprocessing done column by column in scattered notebook cells is difficult to
audit and easy to break.
`Pipeline` and `ColumnTransformer` from scikit-learn are useful because they keep the
workflow explicit:

- which columns are numerical or categorical,
- how each branch is transformed,
- and which estimator receives the transformed design matrix.

That structure matters in this course because it keeps preprocessing aligned with the
evaluation rule.
A train/test split or cross-validation fold should carry the preprocessing with it,
not rely on a separately edited dataframe.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students whether a model score should be trusted if the preprocessing was done
once on the full dataset "just for convenience."
:::

## Model choice changes the preprocessing pressure

Different algorithms react differently to the same raw data.

- kNN is sensitive to scale because distances drive prediction,
- logistic regression needs numeric inputs and often benefits from well-behaved
  feature scaling,
- tree-based models are less sensitive to monotone rescaling but still depend on
  sensible handling of missing or categorical variables,
- PCA and k-means can be dominated by unit differences if scaling is ignored.

So preprocessing is not a detached data-cleaning topic.
It is the bridge between the raw dataset and the assumptions built into each model
family.

## Where preprocessing fits in `TEM 0052`

This concept belongs at the start of the course because many later topics assume it
without repeating it in full.

Students need preprocessing logic before they can:

- compare regression and classification baselines fairly,
- trust cross-validation results,
- interpret PCA or clustering outputs,
- and build end-to-end scripts that are reproducible outside the notebook.

## What students should report

When students describe preprocessing in `TEM 0052`, they should usually report:

- which variables were kept, dropped, or grouped,
- how missing values were handled and why,
- how categorical variables were encoded,
- whether scaling was applied and which models required it,
- and how the preprocessing was tied to the train/test or validation workflow.
